question: Did the review adhere to pre-defined objectives and eligibility criteria?
answers: nan

question: Were the eligibility crieria appropriate for the review question?
answers:
the purpose of this article is to summarize all
the current published data on the prevalence of HPV in LS in
order to evaluate whether HPV could possibly play a role in the
development of LS.

Eligible studies met the following criteria: (i) reports concerning
the prevalence of HPV in histologically verified LS, (ii) studies
on the prevalence of HPV in LS associated with invasive genital
cancer, carcinoma in situ, or intraepithelial neoplasia of the
male or female genitalia. Preferably, the papers should provide
information on the distribution of specific genotypes of HPV in
LS, although this information was not always available. Articles
not providing information on specific HPV genotypes were also
included because of the low number of articles on the subject.
Included studies had to provide clear information on HPV
detection and genotyping methods. Meta-analyses and reviews
were not included.

question: Were any restrictions in eligibility criteria based on sources of info appropriate (e.g. publication status or format, language, availability of data)?
answers: Only English-language literature was included in the search.

question: Did the search include an appropriate range of databases/ electronic sources for published and unpublished reports?
answers:
The databases MEDLINE (PubMed) and Embase
were searched

question: Were methods additional to database searching used to identify relevant reports?
answers:
Sources
A systematic literature search up to September 23, 2016, was
performed. The databases MEDLINE (PubMed) and Embase
were searched using a combination of the following MESH
terms or Emtree words: "lichen sclerosus", "etiology", "HPV",
"human papillomavirus", "vulvar cancer", "vulvar intraepithelial
neoplasia", "VIN", "penile cancer", "penile intraepithelial
neoplasia", and "PeIN".
Only English-language literature was included in the search.

question: Did the synthesis include all studies that it should?
answers:
A total of 27 papers published from 1991 to 2016 were
included in this study

In only eight of 27 (30%) studies were patients with LS to be
completely negative for HPV.8,28-34 In the 18 studies with HPVpositive
patients, the prevalence of HPV ranged from 4 to
80%

Of the 19 papers that detected HPV DNA in LS lesions, 14 provided
information on specific genotypes.10,12

question: Were all pre-defined analyses reported or departures explained?
answers: nan

question: Was the synthesis appropriate given the nature and similarity in the research questions, study designs and outcomes across included studies?
answers:
In the 18 studies with HPVpositive
patients, the prevalence of HPV ranged from 4 to
80%.10-27,35 A calculation of the median, which allowed elimination
of outliers, showed that the overall prevalence of HPV in
LS is 22%. By further dividing the studies into subgroups based
on gender and calculating the gender-specific median, the
prevalence of HPV is 8% (range 0-52%) in female patients with
LS and 29% (range 0-80%) in male patients with LS, suggesting
a stronger correlation in men than in women.

Looking at the HPV prevalence in LS not associated with
neoplasia, 6/17 (35%) study populations were completely negative
for HPV. On the other hand, only 2/12 (17%) study populations
of LS associated with neoplasia were completely negative
for HPV DNA. However, the difference is less apparent when
medians are calculated, as the median of HPV in LS not associated
with neoplasia is 21% (range 0-70%) compared to 22%
(range 0-80%) in LS associated with neoplasia

question: Did the interpretation of findings address all of the concerns identified in Domains 1 to 4?
answers:
This review demonstrated a prevalence of HPV in LS patients
ranging from 0 to 80%. The diverging detection rates are probably
due to the small sample sizes in most of the reviewed
papers and the use of different techniques for detection of HPV.
